one_line_takeaway: Certified unlearning that only matches a re-trained model on original inputs is not enough: adversarially perturbed forget samples can still be re-recognized ('residual knowledge'), a leakage the paper proves is geometrically inevitable and mitigates with a fine-tuning method (RURK) that penalizes re-recognition of perturbed forget samples.
key_finding: Residual knowledge is prevalent across essentially all existing unlearning methods, and RURK is the only method that both preserves standard utility and drives residual knowledge toward the ideal value of 1. On CIFAR-10 at a small perturbation norm (~0.03), over 7% of forget samples still exhibit residual knowledge in a standard unlearned model. RURK achieves the smallest Average Gap to the Re-train reference in every scenario: 2.60 on small CIFAR-5 (vs NTK 3.23, CR 10.06, Fisher 9.49) and 2.65 on CIFAR-10 (vs NegGrad+ 2.71, SCRUB 4.84, NGD 6.27, GA 21.25, SSD 20.65). Crucially, methods can look good on conventional metrics yet still leak: NTK matches RURK on Avg. Gap but keeps residual knowledge above 1; NegGrad+ ties RURK on Avg. Gap but has a much shorter re-learn time (implying retained forget knowledge); GD and CF-k retain residual knowledge as high as the Original model; while GA and SSD show residual knowledge below 1 (over-unlearning) but crater test accuracy below 90%. RURK keeps residual knowledge near 1 for tau<0.01 and suppresses it at larger tau, and this advantage persists at ImageNet-100 scale (ResNet-50), where GD and SSD still leak.
core_contribution: The paper identifies and formalizes a novel privacy vulnerability in approximate machine unlearning that it calls 'residual knowledge': even when an unlearned model M(A(S),S,Sf) is certified statistically indistinguishable from a from-scratch re-trained model A(Sr) on the original forget samples, slightly (adversarially) perturbed forget samples may still be correctly classified by the unlearned model when the re-trained model fails, revealing that traces of the forgotten data persist in the model's local decision-boundary neighborhood. The authors' OWN contributions are three-fold: (1) two theoretical results — Proposition 1, that adversarial examples crafted against a specific model realization degrade (epsilon,delta)-indistinguishability (increasing distinguishability by a factor of two with probability 2*delta/(1-e^-epsilon)), and Proposition 2, that using the isoperimetric inequality on the unit sphere S^{d-1}, disagreement between the two models within a perturbation ball B_p(x,tau) is INEVITABLE in high-dimensional input spaces; (2) a formal, Monte-Carlo-estimable metric of residual knowledge r_tau((x,y)) = Pr[m(x')=y]/Pr[a(x')=y] for x' sampled from B_p(x,tau), where r_tau>1 signals the unlearned model re-recognizes perturbed forget samples more than the re-trained one; and (3) RURK (Robust Unlearning against Residual Knowledge), a fine-tuning objective that adds a regularization term penalizing the model for correctly classifying vulnerable perturbations (found via FGSM/PGD-style attacks) of forget samples while a retain-set loss preserves utility. The 11 unlearning algorithms it compares against (CR, Fisher, NTK, GD, NGD, GA, NegGrad+, EU-k, CF-k, SCRUB, SSD) are prior work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: An adversary who can query an unlearned (deployed) model and craft imperceptible adversarial perturbations of a candidate forget sample. Even though the deployment satisfies certified (epsilon,delta)- or Renyi-unlearning guarantees on the original data, the adversary exploits residual knowledge: if small perturbations of a sample are still correctly recognized by the unlearned model (whereas a re-trained model would fail), this both distinguishes unlearned from re-trained models and leaks that the sample was in the original training/forget set, defeating the intent of the 'Right to be Forgotten' (GDPR). The paper's broader-impact example is a biometric opt-out (palm/face payment): residual traces could let an attacker craft perturbed inputs the system still accepts, enabling unauthorized access. The paper also notes this side-channel (minimal perturbation norm needed to re-recognize a sample) could strengthen membership-inference attacks against unlearned models.
limitations: The authors themselves note: (1) full control of adversarial disagreement over all perturbations in B_p(x,tau) is computationally infeasible — as hard as achieving perfect adversarial robustness; (2) because the re-trained model a~A(Sr) is unavailable during unlearning, RURK can only bound ONE side of residual knowledge (enforcing r_tau(Sf) <= 1, not r_tau = 1); (3) there is a fundamental indistinguishability-vs-robustness trade-off — increasing tau in RURK's regularizer enlarges the adversarial space, making the loss less smooth / higher-Lipschitz and thus increasing the certification epsilon (more distinguishable). Reviewer-noted scope limits: Proposition 2's cleanest form assumes data on the unit sphere (generalized only informally to the unit cube); experiments are confined to vision classification (CIFAR-5/CIFAR-10/ImageNet-100, ResNet-18/50), unlearning a fixed 50% of a single class; generative/LLM unlearning and true MIA exploitation are left as future work rather than demonstrated.

datasets_used: small CIFAR-5 (reduced CIFAR-10: first 5 classes, 200 train + 200 test images per class), ResNet-18
ImageNet-100 (100-class subset of ImageNet-1k, 1,300 images per class), ResNet-50
unlearning setting: only 50% of class 0 unlearned (random-sample unlearning); Original and Re-train trained from scratch without pre-trained weights
quantitative_results: Over 7% of CIFAR-10 forget samples exhibit residual knowledge at perturbation norm ~0.03
Average Gap to Re-train (lower better) small CIFAR-5: RURK 2.60, NTK 3.23, Fisher 9.49, CR 10.06, Original 7.82
Average Gap to Re-train CIFAR-10: RURK 2.65, NegGrad+ 2.71, SCRUB 4.84, NGD 6.27, CF-k 8.16, GD 8.22, Original 8.34, EU-k 7.74, SSD 20.65, GA 21.25
RURK on CIFAR-10: Retain Acc 99.55%, Unlearn Acc 14.63%, Test Acc 92.60%, MIA Acc 18.20%, Re-learn Time >30 epochs (Re-train reference: Unlearn 9.47%, Test 93.30%, MIA 22.50%, Re-learn 17.33 epochs)
RURK on small CIFAR-5: Retain 99.52%, Unlearn 5.67%, Test 93.83%, MIA 33.33%, Re-learn 2.00 epochs
GA CIFAR-10 Unlearn Acc 61.37% but Test Acc 85.98%; SSD CIFAR-10 Unlearn Acc 66.45% but Test Acc 88.59% (both over-forget, residual knowledge <1)
Residual knowledge estimated with c=100 Gaussian (p=2) perturbation samples over varying tau; RURK keeps r_tau near 1 for tau<0.01; Re-train has r_tau=1 by definition; Original consistently r_tau>1
RURK hyperparameters used: tau=0.03, v=1, making its cost comparable to GD/NGD/NegGrad+; results averaged over 3 trials
baselines_compared: CR (Certified Removal, influence-function one-step Newton update) - small CIFAR-5
Fisher (Fisher information matrix on retain set) - small CIFAR-5
NTK (Neural Tangent Kernel linearization) - small CIFAR-5
GD (Gradient Descent fine-tune on retain set)
NegGrad+ (GD on retain + GA on forget)
Reference anchors: Original (full-data model) and Re-train (from-scratch on retain set)
